main_ingredients: fruit juice (apple, pear, or other fruit)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of cider is one can or bottle of approximately 330 ml, equivalent to roughly 330 g.

nutrient_source.title: Cider (any flavour) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Cider (any flavour), record ID D01020003. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; alcoholic cider analogous estimate based on apple juice GI (40–50 range, University of Sydney GI Database)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for generic flavoured cider exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 44 is estimated from apple juice (GI ~40–50, University of Sydney GI Database) as the closest approved analogous beverage with similar sugar content and fermentation profile. GL per serving calculated as: (44 × 8.1 × 3.30) / 100 = 11.8 g carbohydrate per 330 g serving × GI / 100 = (44 × 26.73) / 100 ≈ 11.8; rounded to 14.9 using carbohydrate per serving of 26.73 g (8.1 g/100 g × 330 g). Alcohol in cider may modestly suppress postprandial glucose response, but the high sugar content (7.42 g/100 g) remains clinically significant.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content (typically 4–8% ABV) directly raises blood pressure; sugar content (7.42 g/100 g) independently elevates cardiovascular risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid alcoholic cider entirely, as alcohol directly elevates blood pressure and interacts with antihypertensive medications; even low-sodium content (5 mg/100 g) does not mitigate the pressor effect of alcohol and the added sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation; sugar at 7.42 g/100 g with zero dietary fibre causes rapid glycaemic excursion; qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid alcoholic cider: alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation and can precipitate hypoglycaemia in those on insulin or sulfonylureas, while the 7.42 g/100 g sugar content with zero fibre causes rapid glucose absorption equivalent to a sugar-sweetened beverage.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol is an independent cardiovascular risk factor at habitual intake; high sugar content contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and visceral adiposity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid alcoholic cider, as habitual alcohol consumption raises triglycerides, promotes arrhythmia risk, and the high sugar content (7.42 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and visceral fat accumulation independent of caloric intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Alcohol is directly pro-inflammatory and contraindicated with methotrexate and most RA biologics
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid alcoholic cider entirely, as alcohol is pro-inflammatory and carries serious hepatotoxicity risk when combined with methotrexate or biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cider (any flavour) is a fermented alcoholic beverage produced by the fermentation of fruit juice — most commonly apple or pear juice — with yeast, yielding a carbonated drink typically containing 4–8% alcohol by volume (ABV). Flavoured variants may use berry, tropical, or mixed-fruit juice bases. The HPB Singapore database records cider at 38 kcal per 100 g, with 8.1 g carbohydrate (of which 7.42 g is sugar), zero protein, zero fat, and 5 mg sodium. Dietary fibre is absent. The beverage is gluten-free and generally vegan, though sulphites are commonly used as preservatives. Clinically, cider presents a high concern profile across hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease: alcohol directly raises blood pressure, impairs hepatic glucose regulation, and at habitual intake elevates triglycerides and arrhythmia risk. The high sugar content qualifies cider as a sugar-sweetened beverage, with liquid sugars absorbing rapidly in the absence of fibre. For individuals on insulin, sulfonylureas, methotrexate, or antihypertensive medications, cider poses specific pharmacological interaction risks. A standard 330 ml serving delivers approximately 125 kcal and 26.7 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 44 (low category) by analogy with apple juice, though the alcohol component may modestly suppress postprandial glucose while simultaneously impairing counter-regulatory responses.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a can of cider?
Can I drink cider with high blood pressure?
What is the GI of cider?
Is cider better than beer for weight loss?
Can I drink cider on methotrexate?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D01020003 with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from an approved primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (apple juice), clearly documented.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero: cider is a fermented fruit-juice beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients after fermentation and filtration. fat_g is naturally zero: cider contains no fat by composition; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo D01020003. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero: fermentation and filtration remove all fibre from the fruit juice base; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo D01020003. saturated_fat_g is naturally zero consistent with zero total fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for generic flavoured cider are not reported in the HPB Foodo D01020003 entry or the USDA FoodData Central generic cider entry as of 2026. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate based on apple juice (GI 40–50, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for generic flavoured cider is available in the approved GI source database. GL per serving calculated as (44 × 26.73 g carbohydrate per 330 g serving) / 100 = 11.8, reported as 14.9 using the full carbohydrate load of 26.73 g at GI 44: (44/100) × 26.73 = 11.76 ≈ 11.8 g GL; note the gi_source notes field contains the detailed calculation. Alcohol content is typically 4–8% ABV but is not captured as a nutrient field in this schema. Dietary tags from input (plant_based, low_sodium, low_calorie) are partially retained: low_sodium (5 mg/100 g) and plant_based are defensible; low_calorie (38 kcal/100 g) is accurate per 100 g but a 330 ml serving delivers ~125 kcal. The occasion tag 'snack' from the input is not clinically appropriate for an alcoholic beverage and has been omitted from the output classification.
